data.title: Don't Put All Your REIT Eggs In One Basket
data.description:
I began my career in commercial real estate - not behind a desk, but in the field.

As a developer, I learned how to create value from the ground up. In the 1990s, I analyzed balance sheets and income statements for dozens of retailers, including Dollar General (DG), Advance Auto (AAP), CVS (CVS), PetSmart, and many others. That’s when I learned the single most important lesson in investing: that the cost of capital drives everything.

Like many investors, I made the mistake early on of concentrating too much capital in one partnership… one geography… one property sector…and one asset class.

And I paid the price.

That experience shaped my philosophy - and ultimately led me to real estate investment trusts (REITs), where diversification, liquidity, and transparency create a more durable wealth creation model.

Today, as I return to Seeking Alpha, I want to focus on something timely:

Why REITs deserve a bigger allocation today—and where to find the widest moats.

Data Centers: Mission-Critical Infrastructure

Data centers are the backbone of the digital economy, where AI, cloud, and enterprise demand are creating a surge in need for power, connectivity, and secure infrastructure. Wide Moat drivers for the sector include:

  • Switching costs are enormous
  • Power access is scarce
  • Development is complex and capital-intensive
  • Long-term enterprise tenants

I’ve been covering data centers for over a decade, starting with Digital Realty (DLR) that IPO’d in 2004, the CyrusOne spin from Cincinnati Bell, and the Equinix (EQIX) REIT conversion in January 2015.

At one time, I also covered QTS Realty, CoreSite, and DuPont Fabros, all of which were acquired: QTS was sold to Blackstone for $10 billion in 2021, CoreSite was sold to American Tower for $10.1 billion in 2021, and DuPont Fabros was sold to Digital Realty for $7.5 billion in 2017.

CyrusOne was also acquired by KKR and Global Infrastructure for $15 billion in March 2022 and was one of my best picks ever, returning around 500% over 9 years (IPO’d at $19 per share and taken out at $90.50 per share, a strong validation of the REIT model.

I’ve also watched Iron Mountain’s (IRM) transformation from a pure physical records storage company (warehouse full of paper documents) into a hybrid storage model powered by data centers and digital infrastructure.

In November 2020, I argued that shares would “mushroom,” recognizing the “sticky” customer base, suggesting 40% annualized total return potential. Since that article, shares have returned 35% annually, that’s an exceptional compounding rate - well into “elite” long‑term performance territory.

Looking back at data center REIT performance over the last decade (2016-2025), as shown below, data centers have generated annual returns of 14.5%, the second-best performance across all REIT categories.

And so far in 2026, Data Center REITs have generated superior returns (as shown below) because, as I explained previously, they sit at the center of the AI + cloud + digital infrastructure boom.

The biggest catalyst, of course, is AI compute demand, in which hyperscalers like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google are leasing space at record levels. This demand has, in turn, created “supply scarcity” in key data center markets like Northern VA, Dallas, and Santa Clara.

In addition, as I noted earlier, private capital has validated the sector, and other REITs such as Realty Income (O), Prologis (PLD), and COPT Defense Properties (CDP) are acquiring data center assets.

Of the three “pure play” data center REITs, Equinix offers the best value, now trading at a multiple of 25x P/AFFO. That’s not a bargain whatsoever, recognizing that this REIT, as well as Digital Realty and Iron Mountain, are all expected to grow by around 10% over the next few years.

Hopefully, like me, you jumped onto the train before leaving the station, having recognized that AI demand is accelerating faster than expected.

Industrial & Logistics: The Arteries of Commerce

Industrial real estate is no longer cyclical - it’s structural. E-commerce, supply chain resiliency, and reshoring trends continue to drive demand. Wide Moat drivers include:

  • Infill locations near major metros
  • High land constraints
  • Sticky tenants
  • Embedded rent growth

As a previous chart highlights, industrial REITs were the best-performing sector from 2016 to 2025, returning 16.2% annually. However, so far in 2026, Industrial REITs have underperformed, generating returns less than 1% YTD, which suggests there’s potential value that can be generated by owning shares within the mission-critical category.

My top picks in the sector include Prologis (PLD), EastGroup (EGP), STAG Industrial (STAG) – all differentiated by design.

Prologis, of course, has a scale advantage, perhaps the closest thing real estate has to a monopoly on global logistics infrastructure. The $170 billion (Enterprise Value) REIT owns 1.3 billion square feet of distribution facilities in the most supply-constrained markets on earth – LA, Inland Empire, NJ ports, Tokyo, London, and even near BMW’s US manufacturing plant in my hometown of Spartanburg, SC

Prologis owns land that can’t be replicated and demand is driven by e-commerce, AI supply chains, and onshoring/reshoring. The REIT isn’t just a warehouse landlord; it’s a logistics ecosystem that creates wealth with its development pipeline ($43 billion), data insights on supply chains, and value-added services (energy, automation, and data centers).

EastGroup is a uniquely-positioned RET that owns 560 high-quality, small-bay industrial properties in the fastest-growing US Sunbelt markets – a niche that’s quietly becoming one of the most durable in REIT-dom. The company focuses on infill industrial markets where there’s tenant demand (population + job growth) and infrastructure (near ports and highways).

Unlike Prologis, which has bigger boxes, EastGroup focuses on smaller tenants that include local distributors, service providers, and e-commerce last-mile users. These “flex-space” tenants move less frequently, depend on proximity, and have fewer alternatives, which creates higher retention and pricing power.

As a result, EastGroup consistently reports strong same-store NOI growth and double-digit leasing spreads. The development pipeline (50% of the portfolio) is also active, which is a key driver for internal growth without overpaying in the open market.

This Mississippi-based REIT doesn’t get the headlines like data centers or AI Plays, but over time it delivers steady dividend growth, consistent AFFO expansion, and lower volatility than many peers…a perfect “sleep well at night” REIT.

Finally, there’s STAG Industrial, another Industrial REIT that I was the first analyst to cover in November 2011. “…because of very little competition in these secondary markets, STAG is able to act like a “bigger fish in a small pond”.”

When I published that article, STAG had a market cap of less than $600 million, and over the course of 15 years, the company has grown to a market cap of over $7 billion, generating a total return of 645% (annualized 15% per year).

STAG’s business model is straightforward: own a simple, repeatable cash‑flow machine that compounds quietly over time. By focusing on secondary markets (across 40 states) and mostly single‑tenant warehouses (500+ buildings) STAG’s highly functional business model benefits from:

  • Lower acquisition costs vs. gateway markets
  • Less competition from institutions
  • Higher initial yields

The “real moat” for STAG is its acquisition engine in which the company purchases properties at attractive cap rates while reducing tenant and geographic concentration. In doing so, STAG has generated consistent and predictable dividend growth, while also reducing the payout ratio (which enhances dividend safety).

In terms of valuation, Prologis is now trading around Fair Value with a P/AFFO multiple of 28x P/AFFO and a dividend yield of 3.4%. Analysts forecast growth of 8% this year and 9% in 2027, which suggests dividend growth is likely.

My suggestion here is to wait for a pullback and consider EastGroup instead; shares are trading at 27x (normal value is 29x) with a dividend yield of 3.3%. Analysts are forecasting 10% growth this year and 7% in 2027, translating into a total return estimate of 15% to 20% over the next 12 months.

STAG is trading at 17x, a tad below the company’s normal multiple of 18x. The dividend yield is 4.2%, and growth is forecasted at a modest 2% this year and 6% in 2027. I would look to own STAG if shares dip below 5% or more, which makes EastGroup my top pick in the Industrial sector.

Net Lease: Durable Income in an Uncertain World

When volatility rises, predictability becomes priceless, which is why net lease REITs deserve a place setting as they deliver stable, bond-like income with equity upside. The “wide moat” drivers for the property sector include:

  • Long duration lease contracts (15 to 20 years, plus options)
  • Tenants are responsible for expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance)
  • Diversified tenant and geographic mix
  • Strong supply of products (sale-leasebacks, build-to-suits, etc.)

My top “sleep well at night” REITs in the net lease sector include Realty Income (O), Agree Realty (ADC), and Essential Properties (EPRT).

Many of you know that I’ve been covering Realty Income for over 15 years here on Seeking Alpha and I’ve been an investor in the business for over twenty years. I’ve watched the moat get wider as Realty Income has used its strong balance sheet and scale to deliver consistent and [predictable dividend growth.

When I first started on Realty Income, the company had just over 2,500 properties with a market cap of around $7 billion and today the platform boasts a market cap of $40 billion with a portfolio of over 15,000 properties (in all 5 states and Europe).

Realty Income has also moved beyond its legacy business model of land-lording for fast-food chains (like Taco Bell) to target casinos, data centers, grocery stores, warehouses, and convenience stores.

Meanwhile, Agree Realty has also become a more disciplined capital allocator by targeting fungible, investment-grade tenants and avoiding bookstores (e.g., Borders Books, which was once a customer) and Topgolf properties.

Credit to CEO, Joey Agree, who has exercised financial discipline to improve Agree’s balance sheet so the company can become more competitive by lowering its cost of capital to generate steady profits and dividend growth.

Essential Properties is also one of my favorite net lease REITs. The company began trading in June 2018, and as I explained in an article (back in 2018), “it’s becoming increasingly evident that M&A is likely in the Net Lease sector, and it is only a matter of time before consolidation transforms the top players into the next Wells Fargo and Bank of America in the Net Lease sector.”

EPRT has certainly become a contender in the net lease space, by focusing on middle-market businesses that are hard to disrupt online (car washes, medical, QSRs, etc..) and are more “needs‑based” (not discretionary retail). EPRT seeks to acquire properties at higher cap rates (than O and ADC) so the company achieves better investment spreads that drive AFFO per share growth.

In terms of valuation, Realty Income is trading at 14.4x P/AFFO (the normal is 17x), with a dividend yield of 5.2% (the company has increased its dividend for 31 consecutive years).

Given the size of the denominator ($88 billion EV) Realty Income has to work harder to grow the business (guided $8 billion in investments this year) which is one reason it has recently secured JV deals with Apollo and GIC.

Analysts are forecasting 3% growth in 2026 and 2027, and while that’s less than we’ve seen over the company’s history (5%-ish), I’m attracted to the 20% annual return made up of price appreciation (12%), dividend yield (5.2%), and growth (3%).

Agree is also attractive, with a P/AFFO multiple of 17.5x (normal is 19x) and dividend yield of 4.1%. Analysts expect 5% growth this year and next, which suggests the Michigan-based landlord's shares could also return around 20% over the next 12 months (4.1% + 5% + 10%).

EPRT has pulled back to a multiple of 17x (normal is 18x) and while the dividend yield of 4.0% is lower than O and ADC, the payout ratio is lower (around 65%).

EPRT provided 2026 AFFO per share guidance of $1.99 to $2.04, which implies a growth rate of about 7% at the midpoint (higher than O and ADC). I forecast shares could return 15% to 20% over the next 12 months.

Specialty REITs: Where the Moats Get Wider

This is where inefficiencies create opportunity.

Specialty REITs often operate in niche markets with limited competition, high barriers, and unique pricing power. I’ll highlight a few of my favorites and why they deserve shelf space within an intelligent REIT portfolio.

I like billboards, and I consider these highly regulated assets to be monopolies in plain sight (due to limited supply). Warren Buffett obviously agrees with me because his company Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) took a position last year worth around $140 million.

I own a few parcels of land leased to Lamar, and in my town, in which the REIT operates like a local monopoly, because they have the best locations. Although shares have pulled back to around 15.3x P/AFFO (normal is 15x) I would wait on a better entry point (14x is idea). The dividend yield is around 5% with growth estimates of 5% this year and next.

Cold storage is one of the most misunderstood sectors right now - and mispriced – due in large part to high construction and operating costs and oversupply. However, it’s due to complexity and oversupply that shares in Americold Realty (COLD) and Lineage (LINE) have been beaten down.

Of these two, I like AmeriCold given its higher 8% yield (LINE’s yield is 6.4%) and lower multiple (COLD trades at 8.3x and LINE trades at 10.1x). I caution you, however, that it may take a few more innings to see shares move up until capacity catches up with demand and utilization tightens.

I’m sure you’ve all heard the news that President Trump is looking to ban institutional investors from the single-family rental sector. I recently wrote an OpEd on the subject in which I explained that

“If policymakers want to improve affordability, the policy prescription is straightforward: increase supply. That means streamlining zoning and permitting, reducing regulatory barriers and encouraging private capital formation.”

Simply put, I believe REITs should be carved out of the proposed legislation because “from both a market and policy standpoint, it is unlikely to improve affordability - and could ultimately exacerbate the housing shortage investors are already pricing in.”

The two larger players in the specialty residential sector are Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH). Both are cheap, INVH trades at 15.7x (normal is 24x) and AMH trades at 17x (normal is 25x).

Keep in mind, headline risk is high, as the media narrative (Wall Street vs. Main Street) has created multiple compressions. I find it highly unlikely that policymakers will force a liquidation and disrupt current lease structures, as that would destabilize housing, which is politically risky.

Own shares now (dividend yield around 4.5%) and wait it out, I think the worst is already priced in.

Manufactured Housing may be the most durable moat in all of REIT-dom because of (1) extremely low turnover (sticky tenants), (2) high demand (silver tsunami), and (3) limited supply (zoning restrictions). Also, the so-called “affordability crisis” has created strong structural demand.

Within this sub-sector, I like Equity LifeStyle (ELS), which trades at 24.2x P/AFFO (normal is 30x) and offers a dividend yield of 3.4%. Analysts forecast growth of around 5%, and I’ve targeted shares to return around 20% over the next 12 months.

Finally, I want to highlight the casino REIT sector, which is also a sub-category of the Net Lease REIT sector.

Casinos are location‑dependent, capital‑intensive assets in which the licenses are hard to obtain. Operators invest billions into properties, and moving isn’t an option, which makes tenants highly committed and low risk to vacate.

Given the slowdown in Las Vegas and more recent activism at Caesars, VICI's shares have pulled back to 11.5x P/AFFO (the normal is 15.3x). While I can’t argue with the softness we’re seeing in Las Vegas, the future of “Sin City” looks bright as Allegiant Stadium will officially be home to the 2029 Super Bowl.

I’m happy to hold onto VICI’s 6.5% dividend and 3% growth until shares reprice, which could result in returns in excess of 20% during the next 12 months.

Final Thoughts: Build the Moat, Then Allocate Capital

REITs are not a monolith. They are a collection of businesses - some average, some exceptional and the key is to identify durable cash flows, strong management teams, competitive advantages, and intelligent capital allocation. As much as I love REITs, I would not recommend putting all your eggs in one basket.

But today…I believe it’s time to overweight REITs.

Because when you combine:

  • Attractive valuations
  • Reliable income
  • Structural demand drivers

…you get an asset class that deserves a larger seat at the table.

I’m excited to be writing again on Seeking Alpha and reconnecting with this community.

As always, I welcome your thoughts, ideas, and feedback.

Happy Easter!

Author's Note: Brad Thomas is a Wall Street writer, which means he's not always right with his predictions or recommendations. Since that also applies to his grammar, please excuse any typos you may find. Also, this article is free: Written and distributed only to assist in research while providing a forum for second-level thinking.

data.title: A Hot CPI Report Could Force A Major Market Repricing
data.description:
The March CPI is expected to be a real barn burner. It is no mystery at this point as to why WTI Crude oil (CL1:COM) and gasoline prices are surging. The part with less clarity is how hot it will be and what it means for markets.

Analysts forecast the March headline CPI to rise 0.9% month over month, up from 0.3%. Headline CPI is expected to rise to 3.3% year-over-year. Even core CPI isn't expected to be great, rising to 0.3% m/m up from 0.2% in February, while rising 2.7% y/y from 2.5% y/y.

Gasoline Prices Have Surged

According to the EIA, all grades of conventional gasoline rose to $3.914 by March 31, from $2.89 in late February, a massive jump of about 35%. This is important because gasoline had a 2.812% weighting in the CPI report in February. At that kind of weight, gasoline doesn’t need to do much to move the index. A 15% increase adds roughly 0.4% to CPI, and depending on how the monthly average plays out, the contribution could approach 0.5%–0.6%. More importantly, if these effects are not fully realized in March and gasoline prices remain elevated into April, the inflation impulse could extend further.

Additionally, gasoline prices are now trading near the upper end of their historical range seen during other inflationary regimes, such as the 2007 to 2008 period and 2022, and may be consistent with a longer-term inflationary regime if they do not come down quickly. Additionally, the higher gas prices between 2010 and 2016 certainly had a knock-on effect on overall economic growth.

The online prediction website Kalshi, which has in the past been a reliable source for CPI reports, is even pricing in a 0.9% m/m increase in inflation for March and 3.3% on a year-over-year basis, in line with analysts' estimates. Core CPI is expected to come in at 0.3% m/m and rise to 2.7%, also in line with analysts' estimates. The CPI swaps market is also pricing in a similar rise in the market y/y inflation rate, from 3.3% in March to around 3.9% in April.

No Rate Cuts

Due to the spike in energy prices and the inflation it is expected to bring, markets are already repricing the path of monetary policy. Currently, Fed Fund Futures are trading at 3.63%, effectively pricing out any rate cuts.

But plenty of questions remain about how things will unfold in the coming weeks and how quickly higher oil prices will begin to feed through the rest of the U.S. economy. Most regional Federal survey data points did not show a meaningful jump in their prices paid indexes in March, but the ISM Manufacturing Index did see its prices paid index jump to 78.3, its highest reading since the summer of 2022. The ISM services report will be released on Monday, and the price paid index could add some more color to inflationary pressures across the economy. Clearly, though, the sharp rise in the manufacturing sector suggests the rising costs are filtering through to the economy quickly. Let us not forget that prior to the rise in oil prices, metals such as copper, gold, platinum, palladium, and silver had also been rising, suggesting future inflationary pressures.

Whether the CPI report comes in hotter than expected is a strong possibility, given how quickly oil prices have risen. Simply given the rise in gasoline alone, the month-over-month reading is going to be firm; it just depends on how hot, and, when factoring in the other parts of the energy complex, which altogether have a weighting of more than 6% in the index, the impacts could be much more pronounced. Again, how hot it gets will depend on how quickly these higher energy costs feed through to the rest of the economy.

The market will be very quick to reprice the risk around the Fed having to act and push rates higher, even if there is a new Fed chair. It will be hard for a new Fed chair to cut rates if the rest of the committee is not in alignment. Simply looking through rising energy prices would also be a big mistake.

Obviously, if CPI comes in cooler, the market can rejoice, rates can fall, and the risk-on theme can play for a little bit longer.

data.title: 2 Durable Income BDCs That Can Go On Offense
data.description:
Because of the heightened volatility and fundamental concerns about private credit (including BDCs), investors who have decided to keep their positions in place or even add a bit are doing so by leaning into defensive names.

By defensive names I mean such BDCs that are well-positioned to weather potential headwinds from rising non-accruals in the overall BDC (BIZD) space (e.g., low PIK, low non-accruals, low exposure to SaaS, low leverage).

It is about taking care of the downside and then letting the upside do its thing when the negative sentiment fades. While in the process of waiting, 10%+ dividends can be collected.

There is absolutely nothing wrong with this kind of approach. In fact, this is how I do it and what I have been recommending for BDC investors who seek durable cash flows and long-term NAV stability.

However, there is a saying in sports: the best defense is a good offense.

In the BDC context, I interpret it as expanding portfolios, which would contribute to incremental NII and NAV per share growth that could, in the worst case, be used as an offset to the "attacks" from potential loan default front.

Namely, the rhetoric is changed from using the built-in cushion and margin of safety as a shock absorber but not even allowing the headwinds to touch the current value and instead letting incremental value (NII, NAV) fight back.

In this particular moment, going on attack mode could be very attractive because of higher spreads, which have recently expanded due to (a) sizeable amounts of liquidity being pulled out of BDCs (less competition) and (b) increased risk in the system (SaaS, recessionary risks).

Now, the worst thing that could happen is if the BDC decided to venture into aggressive portfolio growth from a position of instability and struggling fundamentals.

What we want is to capture both worlds:

  1. Stable fundamentals and solid portfolio (i.e., below-average risk BDCs).
  2. Big firepower to secure growth without aggressive debt taking.

We could also describe it as leveraging stability, where BDCs rely on their established fundamentals to take calculated risks (expanding) without fear of calamity.

Here are my two BDC picks.

#1: Kayne Anderson BDC

Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) is an externally managed, 11.5%-yielding BDC which focus on core middle market lending.

At its core, KBDC is very similar to most other BDCs (e.g., external management, traditional business sector lending, limited focus on equity investments), but there is one huge difference.

The difference lies in the fundamentals and the corresponding earnings quality, which are in significantly better positions than for the peers. The P/NAV of 0.85x, which is above the sector median of 0.72x, nicely proves that.

And the beauty here is that KBDC's fundamental advantages are not just about strong defense but also about pronounced offense. Let me quickly explain.

For example, almost all portfolio loan investments are senior secured first lien, all of them are with financial covenants, and 99% of them are backed by private equity sponsors. I know that these items are not that unique, but still, many peers don't have them (at such a level).

What sets KBDC even more apart from the typical BDC is that its weighted average portfolio loan investment interest coverage and net leverage stand at 2.4x and 4.5x, respectively. Usually, most BDCs carry interest coverage closer to 2.0x and leverage at or slightly above 5.0x.

The results speak for themselves: e.g., Q4 2025 non-accruals (based on FV) were just at 1.4%.

These aspects, in combination with one of the lowest SaaS exposures in space (at ~2% of the loan book), create a solid foundation to consider offense, which is exactly what KBDC can afford.

It is simple. KBDC has one of the least leveraged balance sheets in the sector. Its debt-to-equity is 1.02x, while the average player has it around 1.25x. From today's level, KBDC can easily increase its debt to 1.20x, which is a common target capital structure point that nicely balances risks with return potential.

On top of that, KBDC has a growth-friendly base dividend coverage of 110% (the sector average is 97%), which leaves a decent amount of retained NII generation in the system that could be used together with additional debt to expand the book in a more sustainable manner.

Finally, it is encouraging to see that in the Q4 earnings conference call, the management already had recognized this opportunity of "being greedy when others are fearful":

As the potential for a prolonged AI software dislocation increases, capital will become tougher to raise in private credit. When you add in the perception of undisciplined underwriting causing the potential for increased losses in the upper middle market, we think it is reasonably likely that spreads will widen over the next year or two as investors worry about a credit cycle. We believe that this will actually create a good environment for new originations and an attractive opportunity for KBDC to invest, while other BDCs and direct lending platforms are dealing with their software portfolios

In a nutshell, I would expect growth in NAV and certainly a stable dividend (which is a luxury in the sector) from KBDC going forward.

#2: Hercules Capital

Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC) is an internally managed, 12.6%-yielding BDC with a focus on high-growth venture capital stage lending. Just as in the case of KBDC, here we are talking about robust fundamentals and a well-capitalized capital structure. But what comes on top of that is access to accretive equity financing.

Let's start with the basics.

HTGC has also tilted its portfolio towards senior secured first lien investment, which is the absolute safest area to be in from a BDC's perspective. Most of these loans are backed by private equity or venture capital sponsors.

As opposed to KBDC, the exposure to SaaS is much more meaningful for HTGC. For instance, in Q4 2025, HTGC had 24.3% of its loan book linked to application software names, and the bulk of its equity/warrant positions were located in the SaaS sector.

However, I would consider these positions de-risked because for SaaS names, HTGC targets LTVs that are 20% or lower and with sub-1x level ARR (annual recurring revenue) attachment points.

The non-accruals again speak for themselves. The Q4 was ended with a sector-leading non-accrual statistic of 0.1% (based on FV). The fact that HTGC's NAV has been steadily rising since 2022 (including Q4, when it went up by 0.7% compared to the prior quarter) also should be appreciated.

Speaking of HTGC's firepower to secure growth, we have to consider three angles.

First, HTGC's base dividend coverage is even higher than for KBDC. As of Q4, it was 120% (based on $0.48 per share of quarterly NII). This kind of level definitely allows HTGC to register debt-free (only driven by equity) growth that actually moves the needle.

Second, of course, using retained equity as the only source for growth is not the optimal way to do that. Debt is a much cheaper source of capital. In HTGC's case, we see almost the same debt capacity that KBDC has - i.e., debt to equity of 1.03x. Namely, HTGC has some decent debt capacity that could be fused with retained equity to accommodate growth.

Third, and this is what really sets HTGC apart from KBDC in terms of growth capacity, the BDC's P/NAV is 1.16x, which means that there is an embedded optionality to source fresh equity through additional share issuances. Selling shares above par reduces the equity cost and also introduces accretive tailwinds to existing shareholders.

Granted, share issuances are still more expensive than debt and retained base dividends, so I expect HTGC to tap this opportunity only if the spreads become really elevated (which we will see only in Q1 2026 earnings data points).

Finally, while we are on the "fresh and accretive equity component," it is worth taking into account that it carries 108 positions in different companies through warrants or common equity. This specific portfolio bucket accounts for ~6% of the total loan book. But in practice, when the equity exits happen, they come with significant gains which massively exceed the recorded accounting value.

I would not assign too high a probability for HTGC being able to monetize part of this portfolio in the near term (due to overall market uncertainty, BDC pessimism, and SaaS volatility), but it might eventually become a more important factor when the bullish sentiment returns.

All in all, just as KBDC, HTGC is among those few BDCs which are well-positioned to expand NAV and thus increase NII per share generation (i.e., go on offense) while having a robust portfolio in place (strong defense).

data.title: Payrolls Pacify Stagflation Scare
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets snapped a five-week losing streak this week, while interest rates retreated from multi-month highs, as resilient economic data helped push back against stagflation concerns amid a continuation of the conflict with Iran. Despite another surge in oil prices to the cusp of multi-decade highs, markets found footing as a steady stream of employment data suggested that the U.S. economy remains more resilient than recent volatility had implied. While shy of the immediate ceasefire investors had hoped for, markets welcomed the prime-time address from the White House, which provided some clarity and a timeline for key objectives. Meanwhile, vessel traffic through the Strait of Hormuz improved during the week, though flows remain well below normal levels. Still, risks remained evident after reports that Iranian forces shot down a U.S. fighter jet and damaged another during rescue operations, underscoring the ever-present potential for escalation.

Snapping its longest losing streak since 2022, the S&P 500 rebounded 3.4% this week, climbing back to within 5% of its January record-high. After entering "correction territory" last week, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 led the advance this week with gains of 4.0%. The Mid-Cap 400 and Small-Cap 600 each advanced 3.1%, extending their recent relative resilience amid continued volatility tied to geopolitical developments. Communication services and technology stocks led the rally as investors rotated back into growth-oriented sectors after a first quarter defined by a pronounced growth-to-value rotation that had favored energy, financials, and other cyclical segments. Energy stocks were among the laggards despite the surge in crude prices. Buoyed by the sharp pullback in long-term interest rates from a multi-month high, real estate equities were among the leaders this week, aided by another wave of REIT M&A news. The Equity REIT Index rallied 3.7% on the week, with 18-of-20 property sectors in positive territory, while the Mortgage REIT Index gained 1.4%. Homebuilders and the broader Housing Index also rebounded this week despite a jump in mortgage rates to the highest level since August.

In bond markets, Treasury yields slipped after a four-week climb - breaking the recent tight correlation between yields and oil prices - as investors weighed a relatively solid slate of U.S. employment data against the potential for surging energy costs to weigh on global growth, particularly across more energy-import-dependent European and Asian economies. WTI Crude Oil surged more than 10% on the week to close around $112/barrel, flirting with the prior multi-decade high set in August 2022 of around $115/barrel. The 10-Year Treasury Yield - which last week hit its highest level since last July - dipped 13 basis points on the week to close at 4.30%. Retreating from eight-month highs set last week, the Fed-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield fell 11 basis points to close at 3.80%. Swaps markets price in a median Fed Funds Rate of 3.69% at the end of 2026, implying 0.2 rate cuts by year-end, a more dovish view than the 0.4 rate hikes implied last week. The VIX volatility index dipped after rising to its highest level in a year as turbulence eased following several weeks of sharp swings. Gold prices rebounded this week following an early-year skid, while the US Dollar was steady near ten-month highs.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

The U.S. labor market showed signs of stabilization in March after February’s sharp decline, though the broader picture remains consistent with a “low-hire, low-fire” environment. The critical Nonfarm Payrolls report showed job growth of 178k in March, a solid rebound from the -133k decline in February and well above consensus expectations for roughly +59k. Revisions were mixed, with January revised up to 160k and February revised lower by 41k, leaving the three-month average at roughly 68k, still indicative of a slower pace of job growth than seen earlier in the expansion. The headline U3 Unemployment Rate edged down to 4.3%, though the decline was driven largely by a 396k drop in the labor force, pushing the participation rate down to 61.9% - its lowest level since late 2021. The broader U6 unemployment rate ticked higher to 8.0%, which is on the upper-end of its four-year range. Other labor indicators released this week generally pointed to a steady but subdued hiring environment. ADP Payrolls data showed that private-sector employment rose by 62k in March. Initial Jobless Claims fell to 202k, near the low end of their two-year range, suggesting layoffs remain limited even as hiring slows. JOLTS data showed job openings falling to 6.88 million in February, with the hiring rate dropping to its lowest level since early 2020 and the quits rate holding at 1.9%, indicating workers remain cautious about switching jobs. Challenger data showed that first-quarter job-cut announcements totaled 217k, the lowest for that period since 2022.

The Nonfarm Payrolls report showed that wage pressures continued to ease, with average hourly earnings rising 0.2% in March and 3.5% year-over-year, the slowest annual wage growth since mid-2021. Even more notable, average hourly earnings for nonsupervisory employees - a closely watched gauge of wage pressure across rank-and-file workers - has cooled more significantly in recent months. That measure, which had been running hotter than the broader “all employees” metric in the post-pandemic period and peaked near 7% year-over-year in early 2022 - eased to 3.4% in March, its slowest pace since the early stages of the post-pandemic recovery. The deceleration underscores the broader normalization in wage growth as labor market tightness continues to ease, with fewer job openings and diminished worker bargaining power tempering pay gains across many industries. The ADP report showed similar trends of moderating wage pressures, with pay increases for both job changers and job stayers hovering near four-year lows. Sector-level details again highlighted an uneven labor market. Health care accounted for much of March’s job growth, adding 76k positions, including the return of workers following a strike at Kaiser Permanente. Construction (+26k) and transportation and warehousing (+21k) also rebounded following February declines that were likely influenced by severe winter weather. Leisure and hospitality and manufacturing likewise posted gains, with manufacturing seeing its strongest hiring since late 2023. On the downside, federal government employment declined by 18k while financial activities shed 15k jobs, extending a cooling trend in finance employment.

Other economic data released this week generally reinforced the picture of steady U.S. demand, even as signs of cooling emerged in global growth. Retail Sales rebounded by 0.6% in February, topping expectations for a 0.5% increase and reversing January’s modest decline, suggesting consumers stepped up spending after a weather-impacted start to the year. Excluding autos and gasoline, sales rose 0.4%, while the closely watched control-group measure increased 0.5%, its strongest gain since October and a positive signal for near-term GDP growth. Gains were broad-based across most categories, including clothing, sporting goods, and online retailers, while auto sales rebounded after winter disruptions. Manufacturing data also came in stronger than expected. The ISM Manufacturing PMI rose to 52.7 in March, the highest reading since August 2022, signaling continued expansion in factory activity as production strengthened and supply chains improved. However, the employment component remained in contraction territory. In contrast, economic momentum in Europe softened, with the Eurozone Composite PMI slipping to 50.5 as higher energy costs tied to the Iran conflict weighed on growth. Elsewhere, the Case-Shiller Home Price Index showed that national home prices rose just 0.9% year-over-year in January, continuing a clear moderation in price appreciation. While elevated mortgage rates have weighed on demand over the past two years, the cooling in home price growth has begun to gradually improve housing affordability at the margin.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Cell Tower: Another busy week of M&A news in real estate offered yet another reminder that discounted public market valuations across real estate and infrastructure continue to attract buyers seeking to arbitrage the persistent gap between public market pricing and private market asset values. SBA Communications (SBAC) - the third-largest cell tower REIT - surged over 20% this week after Bloomberg reported that SBAC is exploring a potential sale following preliminary takeover interest from large infrastructure funds. While the report noted that discussions remain early-stage, the scale of a potential transaction would be substantial: SBA carries an enterprise value of roughly $37 billion, including debt, making it one of the largest potential take-privates in the broader real estate and infrastructure universe in recent years. SBAC owns or operates more than 46,000 wireless infrastructure sites globally - including towers, rooftops, and distributed antenna systems - with most revenue derived from long-term leasing arrangements with wireless carriers. Elsewhere, Uniti Group (UNIT) - a former REIT now operating as a C-Corp - jumped over 30% this week on reports that multiple strategic and financial buyers are evaluating pieces of the company. According to industry reports, T-Mobile US is interested in Uniti’s Kinetic fiber-to-the-home business while TPG is evaluating the enterprise fiber segment, with the possibility that the parties could partner on a broader bid for the entire company. Cell Tower REITs have been among the weakest-performing REIT sectors over the past three- and five-year periods, reflecting a confluence of headwinds including U.S. carrier consolidation following the merger of T-Mobile and Sprint, the strategic retreat of DISH Network from the wireless carrier landscape, and mounting competitive pressure from satellite-based internet offerings.

As discussed in our State of REITs report last week, more than a dozen REITs have agreed to be sold or liquidated over the past six months, including eight acquisitions by private equity firms or non-REIT corporations, three REIT-to-REIT mergers, and two announced liquidations, reflecting an accelerating pace of consolidation. We published a note this week discussing this recent "exodus" out of the REIT space and the resulting dearth of new REIT IPOs, postulating that the lack of recent legislative support for the REIT industry may be partially to blame. The two biggest REIT IPO waves (early-2000s and early-2010s) followed intentional policy expansion of the REIT model, consistent with Congress historically treating REITs as a public-interest vehicle - a way to democratize ownership of commercial real estate. Legislative and regulatory support mattered - exemplified by the TRS and RIDEA legislation in the mid-2000s and a series of rulings in the mid-2010s from the Internal Revenue Service expanding what qualifies as “real estate” - changes that helped unlock entirely new REIT sectors (towers, data centers, SFR, infrastructure) and fueled major IPO waves. But over the past decade, policy momentum has largely disappeared - and in some cases reversed. Meanwhile, persistent NAV discounts have become a defining feature of the sector. That raises a bigger question: are public REIT discounts reflecting, in part, the absence of policy support that historically encouraged real estate to move into public markets?

The question has come into particularly sharp focus in the single-family rental ("SFR") sector, where several policymakers have proposed legislation that would restrict or outright ban institutional investors from acquiring single-family homes. Historically, the precedent in Washington was often to “throw a bone” to the public markets - granting REITs some structural advantage over private capital in the interest of promoting transparency, liquidity, and broader investor access. Today, however, the policy conversation often moves in the opposite direction - whether intentionally or not - tilting the playing field toward private ownership rather than publicly listed real estate. Despite the stakes, the current debate has seen surprisingly little organized defense of the public REIT model. The relative silence from groups like Nareit is notable. Perhaps the industry simply grew comfortable after many of the pivotal battles that shaped the modern REIT sector were fought and won by earlier generations. But if the REIT industry isn’t willing to make the case for why transparent, publicly traded ownership should be preferred to opaque private capital, it shouldn’t be surprised if policymakers stop making that case for them. In that sense, the proposed SFR “institutional ban” could prove to be a broader litmus test for the future policy support of the REIT structure itself -and for how much collective “fight” remains across the industry.

Several REITs announced portfolio-level M&A deals this week. Casino REIT VICI Properties (VICI) rallied 4% this week after it announced a deal to buy the real estate assets of Deerfoot Inn & Casino, Great Northern Casino, and two limited-service hotels in Alberta for CAD$200.6M ($144.4M), which will be added to its existing triple-net master lease agreement. Elsewhere, office REIT AH Realty (AHRT) - which is in the process of selling its multifamily portfolio - rallied 10% after it completed the sale of two multifamily notes previously held within its real estate financing platform for $63M, including its investments in Solis North Creek and Solis Peachtree. AHRT also announced that it repurchased 3.6M shares of common stock for $22M at $5.72/share. Meanwhile, Rexford Industrial (REXR) gained 2% this week after it announced that it has sold five properties year-to-date for an aggregate $127.4M, including previously undisclosed transactions in Valencia ($31.0M), Fontana ($14.5M), and Anaheim ($40.7M). REXR used the proceeds to repurchase 5.53M shares of common stock for $200M at a weighted average price of $36.14/share. Apartment REIT AIMCO (AIV) - which is in the process of selling its entire portfolio - announced that it completed the sale of seven Chicago-area apartment properties. The 1,495-unit portfolio was sold for $455M to LaTerra Capital Management and Respark Residential. AIMCO is also under contract to sell three additional properties expected to close in Q2 2026, while continuing to market several remaining assets.

It was also a busy week on the capital-raising front, including a notable equity raise from a highly levered micro-cap REIT that tripled its share count to pay down a portion of its debt. Service Properties Trust (SVC) - a highly-levered externally-managed REIT under the RMR Group suite - dipped nearly 30% this week after raising $500M via a secondary common stock offering of 416.7M shares, priced at $1.20/share. Prior to the issuance, SVC had 166.0M shares outstanding with an equity market capitalization of roughly $250M. SVC intends to use the net proceeds to redeem its $100M 4.95% senior notes due 2027 and/or its $450M 5.50% senior notes due 2027. On the upside, small-cap mall REIT CBL Properties (CBL) rallied nearly 8% after it closed a $176M floating-rate, non-recourse loan secured by three properties, completing the refinancing of its former $634M secured term loan. CBL also declared a $0.175/share special cash dividend for Q1 2026. The special dividend is in addition to the previously declared $0.45 quarterly dividend, and CBL expects that its new quarterly dividend rate of $0.625 will be its quarterly rate going forward. CBL commented, "This action reflects both the strengthening cash‑flow profile of our portfolio and our confidence in the opportunities ahead.” Elsewhere, we saw over $1.5B in bond issuance week: Realty Income (O) priced $800M of 4.75% senior notes due 2033, while Public Storage (PSA) priced $500M of 5.00% senior notes due 2035.

Additional REIT Headline

  • UMH Properties (UMH) reported that occupancy gains and rent increases achieved throughout 2025 and early 2026 have increased April 2026 rental and related charges by approximately 10% compared with April 2025.
  • WP Carey (WPC) provided a business update, noting that it completed $580M of investments in Q1. WPC also amended its credit agreement to replace a €215M term loan repaid in February with a CAD$347M term loan.
  • Americold Realty Trust (COLD) became the subject of an activist campaign launched by Sieve Capital, urging the company to evaluate all strategic alternatives rather than focusing solely on potential asset sales.
  • Cousins Properties (CUZ) signed a long-term lease with Oracle for 116k square feet at its Neuhoff mixed-use development in Nashville, TN with occupancy expected in the second half of 2026.
  • Digital Realty (DLR) closed its inaugural U.S. hyperscale data center fund with $3.25B of equity commitments focused on hyperscale data center development while retaining a 20% ownership interest.
  • Cousins Properties (CUZ) closed a new five-year $1.2B unsecured credit facility, increasing borrowing capacity by $200M
  • American Assets (AAT) amended its credit agreement to increase its revolving credit facility to $500M from $400M.

REIT Credit Ratings

  • S&P raised the credit ratings of CBL Properties (CBL), including its issuer credit rating to “B+” from “B-”, and revised its outlook to stable from negative.
  • S&P affirmed the credit ratings of Realty Income (O), including its “A-“ issuer credit rating and issue-level rating on its unsecured notes and its “BBB” issue-level rating on its preferred stock with a stable outlook.

2026 YTD Performance & 2025 Recap

With one quarter of 2026 in the books, equity REITs have held onto their outperformance over the large-cap benchmarks despite the recent surge in benchmark interest rates. The Equity REIT Index has gained 3.1% this year, while the Mortgage REIT Index has dipped -1.7%, and the Housing 100 Index has declined -6.7%. This compares to the -3.6% decline for the S&P 500, the 3.4% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 4.5% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 14-of-20 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Data Center, Farmland, and Net Lease REITs. At 4.30%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now higher by 14 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is higher by 33 basis points at 3.80%. Rates at the longest end of the yield curve have risen more modestly, with the 30-Year Treasury Yield higher by 3 basis points to 4.87%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is roughly flat this year after notching gains of 7% in 2025. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil has surged by 50% this year to over $110/barrel after dipping 12% in 2025. The US Dollar is higher by 2%. Gold remains higher by 8% despite some early-year weakness, while Bitcoin has dipped nearly 25% after declining 6% in 2025.

Looking back on 2025, real estate equities significantly lagged the major equity benchmarks, notching a fourth-straight year of underperformance versus all of the major equity benchmarks - the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 - for the first time ever. For REITs - the most interest rate-sensitive market segment - the underperformance aligns with notable weakness in ultra-long duration assets amid lingering inflation uncertainty. The REIT sector finished in the basement among the ten major asset classes - its second time in the bottom spot among the past four years and the third time in the past six years. During the preceding decade from 2010-2019, the REIT sector had never finished worse than sixth among the ten major asset classes. As discussed in our State of REIT Nation, REITs have endured a brutal three-plus-year stretch since March 2022, underperforming the S&P 500 by over 70 percentage points, a magnitude that's more than twice as significant as the underperformance gap seen during the 2007-2009 Global Financial Crisis.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

We’ll see another jam-packed slate of economic data in the week ahead, highlighted by several closely watched inflation readings that could shape expectations for the Fed’s policy path in the months ahead. The main event comes on Friday with the Consumer Price Index, which is expected to show a notable reacceleration in headline inflation in March following the recent surge in global oil prices. Headline CPI is projected to rise to 3.4% year-over-year, up sharply from 2.4% in February, which had marked the slowest annual inflation pace in nearly five years. Core CPI - which excludes food and energy - is expected to hold steady around 2.8%, and will be watched closely for signs of whether higher energy prices are filtering into broader goods and services inflation. Earlier in the week, we'll see the delayed February reading of the PCE Price Index, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge. PCE inflation is expected to remain in the upper-2% range, down from the pandemic-era peak near 7%, but still above the Fed’s 2.0% long-run target. Beyond inflation, investors will also monitor the preliminary April reading of Michigan Consumer Sentiment on Friday, particularly the survey’s closely watched 1-year and 5-year inflation expectations, which policymakers view as a key indicator of whether inflation psychology remains anchored. Meanwhile, labor market updates will continue throughout the week with ADP Employment Change data on Tuesday and the latest weekly Jobless Claims on Thursday.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.
